preFix.title: Carving a kuksa interior
preFix.subtitle: Hollowing the cup of a birch kuksa with a spoon gouge and hook knife
preFix.excerpt: A kuksa is carved from a single birch burl or billet. Hollowing the deep cup interior requires a spoon gouge for the initial roughing, then the deep hook knife for the final shape. This tutorial covers the interior work specifically.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the bent-gouge hollowing method; museum-open-access documentation of Sami treen traditions from the National Museum of Finland.

content.text: A
kuksa
is a single-piece drinking cup carved from birch burl or a thick billet. The Sami tradition carved them from burl for the swirling figure and added structural strength, but a well-selected straight
billet

works for a first piece. The exterior shaping is close to a large spoon; the interior is harder. A kuksa cup is 50 to 60 mm deep, and getting the curve of the base right with the deep hook knife requires patience and attention to wall thickness. This tutorial focuses on the interior work after the exterior has been roughed.
Wood
Birch is the Sami standard and the recommendation. Fresh-cut birch from a branch 12 to 15 cm in diameter gives enough material for a kuksa 90 mm tall and 80 mm across. The
blank
is
green

throughout: working green keeps the hollowing forces manageable and allows slow controlled drying of the walls after carving. If burl is available it is worth using for the figure; the methods are identical.
Tools
Carving axe: for roughing the outer cup profile from the blank.
Sloyd knife: for exterior surface refinement and handle shaping.
Spoon gouge, 12 mm: for roughing the bowl interior. The bent shaft reaches into deep cups where a straight gouge bottoms out.
Hook knife, deep: for refining the lower walls and the cup base. The wider, deeper hook radius is essential here; a medium hook knife cannot reach the bottom of a 50 mm deep cup.
Gouge sweep No. 7, 12 mm (optional): for the upper walls where the cup flares slightly.
Slipstones, set: for sharpening the inside radius of the gouges.
Leather strop: strop every ten minutes.
Raw linseed oil for finishing.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Roughing the exterior

From the round billet, split away the bark and the outer sapwood. Mark the cup outline on the end-grain face: a rough circle 80 mm in diameter, centred 20 mm from what will become the handle side. This offset allows enough material for the integral handle. Use the carving axe to rough the cup exterior to an oval, working push cuts from the top down. The cup base at this stage is still flat; the bottom rounding comes later.
Roughing the interior with the spoon gouge

The spoon gouge has a bent shaft that angles the cutting edge upward relative to the handle. Hold the cup in the non-dominant hand with the opening facing up. Push the gouge in from the top with the dominant hand, driving with the palm on the gouge handle. The
gouge sweep

removes material in scooping arcs from the side walls toward the centre base. Work from the outer wall inward, not from the centre outward; removing the centre first leaves thin overhanging walls that are fragile.

Target wall thickness after rough gouging: 8 mm. This leaves plenty of material for the hook-knife refinement. The base should be at least 10 mm thick at the end of the roughing phase; the base is under most stress in use.
Refining the interior with the deep hook knife

After three days of slow drying, the surface of the rough-gouged interior will have lightened. Now the deep hook knife takes the walls from 8 mm to the target 4 to 5 mm. The deep hook knife reaches the base curve where the medium hook knife cannot.

Hold the cup against the chest, opening toward your dominant hand. The deep hook knife is pulled from the base up the wall toward the rim in short arcs. Each stroke is 30 to 40 mm. The blade-arc stays inside the cup interior; the key safety condition is that the knife hand stays below the cup rim on every stroke. If the hand rises above the rim, the pull-cut arc can exit the cup and cross toward the other hand or the face.

Work around the cup in overlapping sections. Check wall thickness frequently by pinching the wall gently: 4 to 5 mm feels slightly springy under light pressure; 3 mm feels thin; 2 mm will flex visibly. Stop at 4 mm. The
strop
every ten minutes.
Handle shaping

The handle is integral to the cup body. It is thicker than a spoon handle: 18 mm wide and 12 mm tall at the grip, reducing to 14 mm wide at the handle end. Shape with the sloyd knife using push cuts along the sides. A small thumb-hole through the handle is traditional on Sami kuksas; drill it with the cordless drill only after the cup has dried for two weeks, to avoid splitting.
Finishing

Allow the carved kuksa to air-dry at room temperature for ten to fourteen days before the first oil coat. Apply raw linseed oil inside and out, let soak for two hours, and wipe off all surplus. Repeat after one day. Allow two more weeks for the oil to cure before drinking from the cup. The total cure period is three to four weeks from the first oil coat.
Variations
Walnut oil finish: hardens to a firmer film than raw linseed and produces a richer colour in birch. Tree-nut allergen note required on any gifted piece.

Kolrosing on the exterior: once fully dried and oiled, inscribe a pattern on the cup exterior with the sloyd knife and rub in ground charcoal. The wax in a light board-butter coat seals the kolrosing and adds lustre.
Left-handed version: the thumb-hole drilled on the other end of the handle; the handle taper reversed.
Care

Rinse with cool water; towel-dry immediately. Never dishwasher, never overnight soaking. Re-oil the interior with a thin coat of raw linseed every six months or whenever the wood looks pale and dry on the inside.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Dry slowly at room temperature and keep the cup out of sun and away from heating. A base crack can be stabilised with raw linseed worked in with a fingertip, but a large crack on the base means the cup is not food-safe for liquids.
attrs.cause: Base dried faster than the walls, creating tension
attrs.symptom: A crack appeared across the base during drying

attrs.fix: The medium hook knife cannot reach the lower third of a 55 mm deep kuksa. Switch to the deep hook knife. If only a medium is available, use the spoon gouge to reach the base and leave the lower-wall finish slightly rougher.
attrs.symptom: The hook knife cannot reach the lower wall of the cup

attrs.fix: Rotate the cup 180 degrees and work the opposite side. Every section of wall must be worked from at least two opposite arc-entry directions to produce an even thickness.
attrs.cause: Arcs worked from one side only
attrs.symptom: The walls feel uneven when you run a finger inside

attrs.fix: Wipe the interior briskly with a dry cloth, then leave open in a warm dry room for another week. If the smell remains, the cup is not yet safe for drinking; repeat the wiping and wait again.
attrs.cause: Thick oil coat not fully wiped off
attrs.symptom: The oil smell persists inside the cup after three weeks

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
